title: It's Not Endorphins (And That's Kind Of The Point)
hook: For years everyone blamed 'endorphins.' The real chemistry is more interesting, and it works better with people.

body.caption:
For years people said running gives you a rush of 'endorphins.' Newer research says that's not quite right. Johns Hopkins Medicine points to your body's own cannabinoid-like chemicals (called endocannabinoids) as the real reason a hard run can leave you feeling calm and happy afterward.

Here's the part that never makes the study: it works even better with people. A shared run, a five-a-side game, a group hike — same chemistry, minus doing it alone.

KeepItUp helps you find a small local game or run with real people nearby. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Clean, unedited photo — no text overlay. The genuine joy on their faces is the payoff; the science fact and product line live entirely in the caption. Source cited in caption: Johns Hopkins Medicine on endocannabinoids and the 'runner's high.'

title: The Study That Should Worry You A Little
hook: Weak social ties predict early death about as strongly as smoking does — that's not a scare tactic, it's a 300,000-person study.

body.caption:
This isn't a scare tactic, it's just what the research says. In 2010, researchers led by Julianne Holt-Lunstad looked at data from over 300,000 people. They found that having weak social connections predicts early death about as strongly as smoking does.

Not exercise. Not diet. Connection.

The good news: it doesn't take much. A weekly game with the same small group of people counts. You don't need a hundred friends — a few real ones and a reason to see them is enough.

KeepItUp helps you find that reason. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Quote/fact overlaid on the photo, dark scrim across the lower third for legibility. Overlay text: 'Weak social ties predict early death about as strongly as smoking does.' Small subtext: 'Holt-Lunstad et al., 2010.' Source: Holt-Lunstad, Smith & Layton, 'Social Relationships and Mortality Risk: A Meta-analytic Review,' PLOS Medicine, 2010. Finished composite baked at the asset path below.

title: Alone We Can Do So Little
hook: Helen Keller wrote this over a hundred years ago. It's still the whole idea.

body.caption:
Helen Keller wrote that line over a hundred years ago, long before anyone had a phone to scroll on, and it still holds up.

It's also, basically, our whole idea. You can want to meet people for years without doing it. Or you can show up to one small local game this week and let 'together' do what it's always done.

KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Quote overlaid on the photo, dark scrim across the lower third, brand type. Overlay text: 'Alone we can do so little; together we can do so much. — Helen Keller.' Correctly attributed, public-domain line from her writing/speeches. Finished composite baked at the asset path below.

title: The Only Personality Test That Actually Works
hook: Six photos and a bio tell you nothing. A bad pass into the bushes tells you everything.

body.caption:
You cannot tell someone's personality from six photos and a two-line bio. You can tell everything you need to know from watching how they react to sending a pass straight into the bushes.

Do they laugh? Blame the wind? Sprint after it like it's life or death? Now you know more about them than three weeks of messaging would ever tell you.

That's the whole idea behind KeepItUp: skip the small talk, go straight to the small game. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Clean photo, no overlay — the joke lives entirely in the caption (native/authentic treatment). Original KeepItUp line: 'You can't tell someone's personality from a bio. You can tell everything from how they react to a bad pass.'

title: Text Back vs Snap Back
hook: Still waiting for a text back? Meanwhile, this whole game took ten minutes to set up.

body.caption:
No shade to anyone currently refreshing their messages — we've all been there. We just think there's a faster way to find out if you like someone: play a game with them this week instead of waiting for three dots to turn into a sentence.

KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access, link in bio.

title: Why Moving Together Works Better Than Moving Alone
hook: Stanford researchers found that moving in sync with someone makes you trust them more. Here's the honest version of that.

body.caption:
Researchers at Stanford (Wiltermuth and Heath, published in Psychological Science) found something simple: when people move in time with each other — even just walking in step — they trust and cooperate with each other more afterward.

It's a small thing that adds up. You don't need to be best friends before a game. Show up and move alongside people a few times, and the trust tends to follow.

KeepItUp helps you find that small local game. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.cta: Open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
body.imageConcept: Blend treatment: a small label tag top-left of the photo on a small dark scrim reading 'THE SCIENCE OF SHOWING UP,' photo otherwise left clean; the full explanation lives in the caption. Source: Wiltermuth, S.S. & Heath, C., 'Synchrony and Cooperation,' Psychological Science, 2009. Finished composite baked at the asset path below.

title: The Year Is Half Over. You Still Have Every Tuesday Left.
trend: July marks the calendar's halfway point — a recurring, brand-safe seasonal beat ('six months in, how's it going') that many creators post about every year. Not a fabricated moment, no real person referenced, no invented traction. Time-sensitive: post in the first half of July while the mid-year framing still reads as timely.
hook: It's July. If your New Year plan to meet more people quietly didn't happen yet, you're not behind.

body.caption:
It's July. If your New Year plan to 'meet more people' or 'get out more' quietly didn't happen yet, you're not behind — most plans like that just need one specific day and place, not another resolution.

Pick an evening this week. There's a small local game or run happening near you already. Go stand next to it.

KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access, link in bio.
